summary: The article explores Socrates' philosophy applied to education, emphasizing that the ultimate goal of learning is the pursuit of virtue. It highlights his dialogical methods—elenchus (refutation) and maieutics—as tools to dismantle false knowledge and encourage self-discovery, arguing that true education is an internal search rather than the mere transmission of facts.

principles.statement: Virtue is knowledge, but it cannot be taught directly; it requires an inner search.
principles.rationale: Virtue involves reflected discernment and inner conversion that individuals must discover for themselves, rather than memorizing it like a multiplication table.
principles.source_quote: virtue is a knowledge and on the other hand it cannot be learned as one learns the multiplication table... it implies a work of inner conversion

principles.rationale: By possessing moral virtue, a person acts in a good and beautiful manner, which inherently constitutes happiness. The absence of virtue is unhappiness.
principles.source_quote: The relationship between virtue and happiness, in Socrates, is an identity relation, then, by virtue, man acts in a good and beautiful manner and therefore is happy

principles.statement: The principle of knowledge is recognizing one's own ignorance.
principles.rationale: To truly learn and become wise, one must first abandon the presumption of knowledge and adopt intellectual humility.
principles.source_quote: The principle of knowledge is to recognize his or her own ignorance; so, who intends to become a sage, make of himself or herself an ignorant

frameworks.name: Elenchus (Refutation)
frameworks.when_to_apply: To rout knowledge presumption and drive the individual to know the essence of a subject, not just its appearance.
steps: Engage in a dialogue with questions and answers concerning a determined theme.
Identify the logical inconsistence or incoherence in the interlocutor's thesis.
Force the rejection of the thesis or affirmation based on this incoherence.
frameworks.source_quote: consists in a dialogue whose questions and answers concerning determined theme, at the end of that one should fatally make option for rejection of some thesis or affirmation, after being found its logic inconsistence or incoherence.

frameworks.name: Maieutics
frameworks.when_to_apply: When educating someone to help them achieve self-knowledge and moral excellence.
steps: Refuse the role of knowledge transmitter.
Awaken, raise doubts, and require research through dialogue.
Stimulate the apprentice to search within themselves to conceive their own thoughts and realize the 'know thyself' principle.
frameworks.source_quote: Human formation is to Socrates maieutics (operation from inside to outside) and a dialogue... which awakens, raises doubts, requires research, drives, problematizes etc by dialogue

mental_models.name: The Educator as an Encourager
mental_models.description: Viewing the teacher not as a holder of knowledge who transmits facts, but as a facilitator who stimulates the student's internal search for self-knowledge and virtue.
mental_models.example: Socrates never claimed to be a sage or master, nor did he charge money; instead, he engaged in dialogue to help individuals reflect and conceive their own thoughts.

heuristics: I know that I know nothing.

signature_quotes.text: Nothing else I do but walk thereabouts convincing you, young and old men, to not caring so hardly your body and riches, but so how to improve as much as possible the soul, telling you that virtue does not come from possessions to men, but from virtue comes possessions and all other particular and public goods
signature_quotes.context: Socrates admonishing his fellow citizens to prioritize virtue over material wealth.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Knowledge belongs to mankind and is free; treating it as a jailed commodity to be sold by specialists deprives humans of a fundamental right.
Hoisting oneself as a sage prevents true learning. One must admit their own ignorance to begin the path to wisdom.

anti_patterns.description: Prioritizing the body and material possessions over the soul.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Material wealth does not produce virtue; rather, true public and private goods are the result of a virtuous soul.
